summary:
A single unit of packed red blood cells can increase hemoglobin concentration significantly, generally between 3% and up to almost 8%, reflecting variable outcomes based on specific patients' conditions; thus, option \(D\) covering both low-end common occurrences and high-range potential effects accurately represents possible results. The precise impact varies due to differing physiological contexts encountered frequently within medical care environments. To summarize:

A single unit of packed red blood cells usually boosts Hematocrit levels by around 3%-almost 8%, depending on individual patient characteristics leading to considerable variance noted amongst studies and cases. This substantial fluctuation underscores why options including multiple endpoints such as choice $D$ are most appropriate when estimating expected changes post-transfusion.
